mn86:1.2: that on one occasion the Blessed One was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery.
mn86:2.1: And at that time in King Pasenadi’s realm there was a bandit named Aṅgulimāla: brutal, bloody-handed, devoted to killing & slaying, showing no mercy to living beings.
mn86:2.2: He turned villages into non-villages, towns into non-towns, settled countryside into unsettled countryside.
mn86:2.3: Having repeatedly killed human beings, he wore a garland [māla] made of fingers [aṅguli].
mn86:3.1: Then the Blessed One, early in the morning, having adjusted his lower robe and taking his bowl & outer robe, went into Sāvatthī for alms.
mn86:3.2: Having gone for alms in Sāvatthī and returning from his alms round after his meal, he set his lodging in order.
mn86:3.3: Carrying his bowl & robe, he went along the road to where Aṅgulimāla was staying. Cowherds, shepherds, & farmers saw him going along the road to where Aṅgulimāla was staying,
mn86:3.4: and on seeing him said to him,
mn86:3.6: for on that road is Aṅgulimāla: brutal, bloody-handed, devoted to killing & slaying, showing no mercy to living beings.
mn86:3.7: He has turned villages into non-villages, towns into non-towns, settled countryside into unsettled countryside.
mn86:3.8: Having repeatedly killed human beings, he wears a garland made of fingers.
mn86:3.9: Groups of ten, twenty, thirty, & forty men have gone along that road,
mn86:3.10: and even they have fallen into Aṅgulimāla’s hands.”
mn86:3.11: When this was said, the Blessed One kept going in silence.
mn86:3.13: A third time, cowherds, shepherds, & farmers said to the Blessed One,
mn86:3.14: “Don’t go along that road, contemplative.… Groups of ten, twenty, thirty, & forty men have gone along that road, and even they have fallen into Aṅgulimāla’s hands.”
mn86:4.1: When this was said, the Blessed One kept going in silence.
mn86:4.2: Then Aṅgulimāla saw the Blessed One coming from afar
mn86:4.3: and on seeing him, this thought occurred to him:
mn86:4.5: Groups of ten, twenty, thirty, & forty men have gone along this road,
mn86:4.6: and even they have fallen into my hands,
mn86:4.7: and yet now this contemplative comes attacking, as it were, alone and without a companion.
mn86:5.1: So Aṅgulimāla, taking up his sword & shield, buckling on his bow & quiver, followed right behind the Blessed One.
mn86:5.2: Then the Blessed One willed a feat of psychic power such that Aṅgulimāla, though running with all his might, could not catch up with the Blessed One walking at normal pace.
mn86:5.3: Then the thought occurred to Aṅgulimāla:
mn86:5.5: In the past I’ve chased & seized even a swift-running elephant, a swift-running horse, a swift-running chariot, a swift-running deer.
mn86:5.6: But now, even though I’m running with all my might, I can’t catch up with this contemplative walking at normal pace.”
mn86:5.7: So he stopped and called out to the Blessed One,
mn86:5.10: Then the thought occurred to Aṅgulimāla,
mn86:5.11: “These Sakyan contemplatives are speakers of the truth, asserters of the truths,
mn86:5.12: and yet this contemplative, even while walking, says,
mn86:6.1: So Aṅgulimāla the bandit addressed this verse to the Blessed One:
mn86:6.5: you say I haven’t. I ask you the meaning of this: How have you stopped? How haven’t I?”
mn86:6.7: once & for all, having cast off violence toward all living beings.
mn86:6.8: You, though, are unrestrained toward beings.
mn86:6.9: That’s how I’ve stopped and you haven’t.”
mn86:6.10: “At long last a greatly revered great seer
mn86:6.11: for my sake has come to the great forest.
mn86:6.12: Having heard your verse in line with the Dhamma,
mn86:6.13: I will go about having abandoned evil.”
mn86:6.14: So saying, the bandit hurled his sword & weapons
mn86:6.15: over a cliff into a chasm, a pit.
mn86:6.16: Then the bandit paid homage to the feet of the One Well-Gone,
mn86:6.17: and right there requested the Going-forth.
mn86:6.18: The Awakened One, the compassionate great seer,
mn86:6.19: the teacher of the world, along with its devas,
mn86:6.21: That in itself was bhikkhuhood for him.
mn86:7.1: Then the Blessed One set out wandering toward Sāvatthī with Ven. Aṅgulimāla as his attendant monk.
mn86:7.2: After wandering by stages he reached Sāvatthī,
mn86:7.3: and there he lived near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery.
mn86:8.1: Now at that time a large crowd of people, loud & noisy, had gathered at the gates to King Pasenadi Kosala’s inner palace, (calling out,)
mn86:8.2: “There is a bandit in your realm, sire, named Aṅgulimāla: brutal, bloody-handed, devoted to killing & slaying, showing no mercy to living beings.
mn86:8.3: He has turned villages into non-villages, towns into non-towns, settled countryside into unsettled countryside.
mn86:8.4: Having repeatedly killed human beings, he wears a garland made of fingers.
mn86:8.5: The king must stamp him out!”
mn86:9.1: Then King Pasenadi Kosala, with a cavalry of roughly 500 horsemen, drove out of Sāvatthī in the middle of the day
mn86:9.3: Driving as far as the ground was passable for chariots, he got down from his chariot and went on foot to the Blessed One. On arrival, having bowed down to the Blessed One, he sat to one side. As he was sitting there, the Blessed One said to him,
mn86:9.4: “What is it, great king? Has King Seniya Bimbisāra of Magadha provoked you, or have the Licchavis of Vesālī or some other hostile king?”
mn86:10.1: “No, lord. King Seniya Bimbisāra of Magadha hasn’t provoked me, nor have the Licchavis of Vesālī, nor has some other hostile king.
mn86:10.2: There is a bandit in my realm, lord, named Aṅgulimāla: brutal, bloody-handed, devoted to killing & slaying, showing no mercy to living beings.
mn86:10.3: He has turned villages into non-villages, towns into non-towns, settled countryside into unsettled countryside.
mn86:10.4: Having repeatedly killed human beings, he wears a garland made of fingers.
mn86:10.5: I am going to stamp him out.”
mn86:11.1: “Great king, suppose you were to see Aṅgulimāla with his hair & beard shaved off, wearing the ochre robe, having gone forth from the home life into homelessness, refraining from killing living beings, refraining from taking what is not given, refraining from telling lies, living the holy life on one meal a day, virtuous & of fine character: what would you do to him?”
mn86:11.2: “We would bow down to him, lord, or rise up to greet him, or offer him a seat, or offer him robes, almsfood, lodgings, or medicinal requisites for curing illness; or we would arrange a lawful guard, protection, & defense.
mn86:11.3: But how could there be such virtue & restraint in an unvirtuous, evil character?”
mn86:12.1: Now at that time Ven. Aṅgulimāla was sitting not far from the Blessed One.
mn86:12.2: So the Blessed One, pointing with his right arm, said to King Pasenadi Kosala,
mn86:12.4: Then King Pasenadi Kosala was frightened, terrified, his hair standing on end.
mn86:12.5: So the Blessed One, sensing the king’s fear & hair-raising awe, said to him,
mn86:12.6: “Don’t be afraid, great king. Don’t be afraid. He poses no danger to you.
mn86:12.7: Then the king’s fear, his terror, his hair-standing-on-end subsided.
mn86:12.8: He went over to Ven. Aṅgulimāla and said,
mn86:12.11: “What is your father’s clan? What is your mother’s clan?”
mn86:12.12: “My father is a Gagga, great king, and my mother a Mantāṇi.”
mn86:12.13: “Then may Master Gagga Mantāṇiputta delight (in staying here).
mn86:12.14: I will be responsible for your robes, almsfood, lodgings, & medicinal requisites for curing illness.”
mn86:13.1: Now it so happened that at that time Ven. Aṅgulimāla was a wilderness-dweller, an alms-goer, wearing one set of the triple robe made of cast-off cloth.
mn86:13.2: So he said to King Pasenadi Kosala,
mn86:13.3: “Enough, great king. My triple robe is complete.”
mn86:13.4: So King Pasenadi Kosala went to the Blessed One and on arrival, having bowed down to him, sat to one side. As he was sitting there he said to the Blessed One,
mn86:13.6: how the Blessed One has tamed the untamed, pacified the unpeaceful, and brought to unbinding those who were not unbound.
mn86:13.7: For what we could not tame even with blunt or bladed weapons, the Blessed One has tamed without blunt or bladed weapons.
mn86:13.9: Many are our duties, many our responsibilities.”
mn86:13.10: “Then do, great king, what you think it is now time to do.”
mn86:13.11: Then King Pasenadi Kosala got up from his seat, bowed down to the Blessed One and—keeping him to his right—departed.
mn86:14.1: Then Ven. Aṅgulimāla, early in the morning, having adjusted his lower robe and taking his bowl & outer robe, went into Sāvatthī for alms.
mn86:14.2: As he was going from house to house for alms, he saw a woman suffering a breech birth.
mn86:14.3: On seeing her, the thought occurred to him:
mn86:14.6: Then, having gone for alms in Sāvatthī and returning from his alms round after his meal, he went to the Blessed One. On arrival, having bowed down to him, he sat to one side. As he was sitting there he said to the Blessed One,
mn86:14.7: “Just now, lord, early in the morning, having adjusted my lower robe and taking my bowl & outer robe, I went into Sāvatthī for alms.
mn86:14.8: As I was going from house to house for alms, I saw a woman suffering a breech birth.
mn86:14.9: On seeing her, the thought occurred to me:
mn86:14.10: ‘How tormented are living beings! How tormented are living beings!’”
mn86:15.1: “In that case, Aṅgulimāla, go to that woman and on arrival say to her,
mn86:15.2: ‘Sister, since I was born I do not recall intentionally killing a living being. Through this truth may there be wellbeing for you, wellbeing for your fetus.’”
mn86:15.3: “But, lord, wouldn’t that be a lie for me?
mn86:15.4: For I have intentionally killed many living beings.”
mn86:15.5: “Then in that case, Aṅgulimāla, go to that woman and on arrival say to her,
mn86:15.6: ‘Sister, since I was born in the noble birth, I do not recall intentionally killing a living being. Through this truth may there be wellbeing for you, wellbeing for your fetus.’”
mn86:15.7: Responding, “As you say, lord,” to the Blessed One, Aṅgulimāla went to that woman and on arrival said to her,
mn86:15.8: “Sister, since I was born in the noble birth, I do not recall intentionally killing a living being. Through this may there be wellbeing for you, wellbeing for your fetus.”
mn86:15.9: And there was wellbeing for the woman, wellbeing for her fetus.
mn86:16.1: Then Ven. Aṅgulimāla, dwelling alone, secluded, heedful, ardent, & resolute, in no long time reached & remained in the supreme goal of the holy life for which clansmen rightly go forth from home into homelessness, knowing & realizing it for himself in the here & now.
mn86:16.2: He knew: “Birth is ended, the holy life fulfilled, the task done. There is nothing further for the sake of this world.”
mn86:16.3: And thus Ven. Aṅgulimāla became another one of the arahants.
mn86:17.1: Then Ven. Aṅgulimāla, early in the morning, having adjusted his lower robe and taking his bowl & outer robe, went into Sāvatthī for alms.
mn86:17.2: Now at that time a clod thrown by one person hit Ven. Aṅgulimāla on the body, a stone thrown by another person hit him on the body, and a potsherd thrown by still another person hit him on the body.
mn86:17.3: So Ven. Aṅgulimāla—his head broken open and dripping with blood, his bowl broken, and his outer robe ripped to shreds—went to the Blessed One.
mn86:17.4: The Blessed One saw him coming from afar
mn86:17.5: and on seeing him said to him:
mn86:17.6: “Bear with it, brahman! Bear with it!
mn86:17.7: The fruit of the kamma that would have burned you in hell for many years, many hundreds of years, many thousands of years, you are now experiencing in the here & now!”
mn86:18.1: Then Ven. Aṅgulimāla, having gone alone into seclusion, experienced the bliss of release.
mn86:18.6: like the moon set free from a cloud.
mn86:18.10: like the moon set free from a cloud.
mn86:18.12: devotes himself to the Buddha’s bidding:
mn86:18.14: like the moon set free from a cloud.
mn86:18.15: May even my enemies hear talk of the Dhamma.
mn86:18.16: May even my enemies devote themselves to the Buddha’s bidding.
mn86:18.17: May even my enemies associate with those people
mn86:18.18: who—peaceful, good— get others to accept the Dhamma.
mn86:18.19: May even my enemies hear the Dhamma time & again
mn86:18.20: from those who advise endurance, forbearance,
mn86:18.23: For surely he wouldn’t harm me,
mn86:18.25: he would attain the foremost peace,
mn86:18.31: Some tame with a blunt stick,
mn86:18.33: But without blunt or bladed weapons
mn86:18.34: I was tamed by the one who is Such.
mn86:18.35: “Doer of No Harm” is my name,
mn86:18.36: but I used to be a doer of harm.
mn86:18.37: Today I am true to my name,
mn86:18.38: for I harm no one at all.
mn86:18.39: A bandit I used to be,
mn86:18.41: Swept along by a great flood,
mn86:18.42: I went to the Buddha as refuge.
mn86:18.46: Uprooted is [craving], the guide to becoming.
mn86:18.47: Having done the type of kamma
mn86:18.48: that would lead to many bad destinations,
mn86:18.49: touched by the fruit of (that) kamma,
mn86:18.53: while one who is wise cherishes heedfulness
mn86:18.56: or to intimacy with sensual delight—
mn86:18.57: for a heedful person, absorbed in jhāna,
mn86:18.59: This has come well & not gone away,
mn86:18.60: it was not badly thought through for me.
mn86:18.63: This has come well & not gone away,
mn86:18.64: it was not badly thought through for me.
mn86:18.65: The three knowledges have been attained;
